description: Test whether rho differs between encoder/decoder families to explain alpha_encoder >> alpha_decoder
embedding_source: dointerv_multi NPZ files (single layers: pythia-160m L12, pythia-410m L3, rwkv L12, bert L10, electra L3)
key_findings: rho~0.45 is architecture-universal: holds for BOTH encoder and decoder families (range 0.418-0.456, CV=3.4%)
Near-simplex geometry is NOT specific to decoder pre-training; it is a general property of categorical representation
rho does NOT explain cross-family alpha differences: bert-base rho=0.456 (identical to decoders) but alpha_emp=4.4x higher
For NLP decoders: formula alpha=sqrt(4/pi)*sqrt(1/(1-rho)) predicts alpha~1.479-1.529 matching LOAO=1.477
For encoders: same formula predicts alpha~1.516-1.530, but empirical encoder alpha is 3.8-7.7 (mismatch)

Conclusion: near-simplex rho explains alpha universality WITHIN decoder family; cross-family alpha differences have a different origin (bidirectional attention? pooling mechanism? MLM vs CLM pre-training?)
note_on_alpha_emp: alpha_emp here uses single-layer dointerv NPZ files, not the LOAO cross-arch alpha. Decoder single-layer alpha (1.9-2.5) somewhat higher than LOAO=1.477; encoder single-layer alpha (3.8-4.4) lower than LOAO alpha_encoder=7.1-7.7. Both consistent with single-layer suboptimality.
